edges.description: Glycine receptor channel dysfunction reduces rapid inhibitory glycinergic signaling.
edges.source: Postsynaptic Glycine Receptor Chloride Channel Dysfunction

edges.description: Reduced GlyT2 function disrupts presynaptic glycine availability for inhibitory signaling.

edges.target: Postsynaptic Glycine Receptor Chloride Channel Dysfunction
Postsynaptic Glycine Receptor Chloride Channel Dysfunction

nodes.description: Clonazepam is the standard symptomatic pharmacotherapy for hyperekplexia, reducing excessive startle and stiffness in most treated genetically confirmed patients.

evidence.snippet: clonazepam is the treatment of choice (95% found it to be efficacious).
GLRA1 | glycine receptor alpha 1 | hgnc:4326 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
Dominant missense mutations in the human glycine receptor (GlyR) alpha 1 subunit gene (GLRA1) give rise to hereditary hyperekplexia.
In a recessive case of hyperekplexia, we found a deletion of exons 1-6 of the GLRA1 gene.
GLRB | glycine receptor beta | hgnc:4329 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
The screening identified recessive and dominant GLRB variants in 12 unrelated hyperekplexia probands.

nodes.description: Failure of glycinergic inhibition in brainstem and spinal startle circuits leaves excitatory startle reflex pathways insufficiently suppressed, producing excessive startle, stiffness, and related neuromotor features.

evidence.snippet: Glycinergic neurotransmission is a major inhibitory influence in the CNS and its disruption triggers a paediatric and adult startle disorder, hyperekplexia.
The child displayed exaggerated startle responses and pronounced head-retraction jerks reflecting a disinhibition of vestigial brain-stem reflexes.

nodes.description: GLRA1 and GLRB variants impair the postsynaptic inhibitory glycine receptor, a ligand-gated chloride channel that normally mediates rapid inhibitory signaling in the brainstem and spinal cord.
nodes.id: Postsynaptic Glycine Receptor Chloride Channel Dysfunction

evidence.snippet: Dominant missense mutations in the human glycine receptor (GlyR) alpha 1 subunit gene (GLRA1) give rise to hereditary hyperekplexia.
These mutations impair agonist affinities and change conductance states of expressed mutant channels, resulting in a partial loss of function.
Following mutation analysis of the human beta-subunit of hGlyR (GLRB) in a cohort of 22 hyperekplexia patients, we provide evidence to confirm that GLRB mutations can cause human hyperekplexia.

nodes.description: SLC6A5 encodes the presynaptic glycine transporter GlyT2. Pathogenic variants reduce transporter localization or glycine uptake, limiting presynaptic glycine recycling needed for inhibitory synaptic transmission.

evidence.snippet: Here we show that missense, nonsense and frameshift mutations in SLC6A5 (ref. 8), encoding the presynaptic glycine transporter 2 (GlyT2), also cause hyperekplexia.
SLC6A5 mutations result in defective subcellular GlyT2 localization, decreased glycine uptake or both, with selected mutations affecting predicted glycine and Na+ binding sites.
This interaction impairs transporter maturation through the secretory pathway, reduces surface expression, and inhibits transport function.
SLC6A5 | solute carrier family 6 member 5 | hgnc:11051 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
Here we show that missense, nonsense and frameshift mutations in SLC6A5 (ref. 8), encoding the presynaptic glycine transporter 2 (GlyT2), also cause hyperekplexia.
In this study, systematic DNA sequencing of SLC6A5 revealed a new dominant GlyT2 mutation
